description:
<title>Network connectivity audit with sort + uniq frequency counting</title>

    <requirement>
        You are a network engineer who has been handed a compressed
        raw-event capture that contains the results of a 10-second ICMP
        connectivity probe.  
        Your job is to determine two things:  

        1. How many times each final firewall disposition (ACCEPT, DROP,
           REJECT) occurred during the probe window.  
        2. For every destination IP that ever suffered a failure
           (DROP or REJECT) you must count how many failures that IP saw.

        To complete the audit you will:

        • De-compress the supplied file  
          “/home/user/network/logs/connection_attempts.csv.gz”.  
        • Extract the sixth comma-separated field
          (the *Status*, e.g. ACCEPT) and the third comma-separated field
          (the *Destination IP*).  
        • Use *only* standard GNU core-utils text tools (cut, sort,
          uniq, awk, grep, etc.)—specifically the frequency-counting
          capability of “sort | uniq -c”.  
        • Create two plain-text reports inside the directory
          “/home/user/network/reports/”.
          If that directory does not exist, create it.

        The reports **must** have the exact names and byte-perfect
        formats shown below or the automated test will fail.

        ------------------------------------------------------------------
        1. /home/user/network/reports/connection_status_frequency.txt

           EXACT format (three lines, no leading spaces, single space
           between columns, Unix LF line endings):

           ACCEPT 6
           DROP 3
           REJECT 1

           • The lines must be sorted in descending numerical order by
             count.
           • If two statuses had the same count you would then sort them
             lexicographically ascending, but in our data no tie occurs.

        ------------------------------------------------------------------
        2. /home/user/network/reports/ip_failure_frequency.txt

           EXACT format (two lines, no header, single space between
           columns, Unix LF line endings):

           8.8.4.4 2
           8.8.8.8 2

           • Only IPs that experienced at least one DROP or REJECT are
             listed.
           • Lines are ordered by:  
             (a) descending numerical failure count, then  
             (b) ascending dotted-decimal IP address.
           • In the sample data both IPs tie at two failures, therefore
             8.8.4.4 comes before 8.8.8.8.

        ------------------------------------------------------------------

        Verification tips (not commands):  
        • To check the first report interactively you could “cat” it and
          pipe to “nl -ba” so you see the exact line order.  
        • To test the second report you could diff it against a
          here-string with the expected lines.  

        When you are satisfied your two files are exactly correct, you
        may consider the job done.
    </requirement>

    <starting_state>
        The following artefacts already exist before you begin:

        1. Directory:  /home/user/network/logs/
        2. File:       /home/user/network/logs/connection_attempts.csv.gz
                       (gzipped text, 10 CSV records)
                       You will have to gunzip (or zcat) it to read the
                       data.

        No /home/user/network/reports/ directory or report files exist
        yet; you must create them.
    </starting_state>

    <hints>
        • Remember that “sort | uniq -c” emits the count in the first
          column preceded by spaces, so you will need to post-process
          (e.g. with awk) to flip the order to “token count” and strip
          extra whitespace.  
        • You can run multiple pipelines in parallel by ending one with
          “&” if you like, but parallelisation is not graded—only the
          final artefacts are.  
        • A fragment such as  cut -d',' -f6  can isolate the Status
          field;  cut -d',' -f3  isolates Destination IP.  
        • Line endings must be Unix LF, not CRLF.
    </hints>
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth data the autograder will use to mark the agent’s work.
        DO NOT reveal anything under this block to the agent!
    -->

    <preexisting_items>
        <!-- The compressed input file that already exists -->
        <file path="/home/user/network/logs/connection_attempts.csv.gz" type="gz">
2023-10-12 09:00:01,10.0.0.5,8.8.8.8,ICMP,0,ACCEPT
2023-10-12 09:00:02,10.0.0.5,8.8.8.8,ICMP,0,ACCEPT
2023-10-12 09:00:03,10.0.0.5,8.8.4.4,ICMP,0,DROP
2023-10-12 09:00:04,10.0.0.5,1.1.1.1,ICMP,0,ACCEPT
2023-10-12 09:00:05,10.0.0.5,8.8.8.8,ICMP,0,DROP
2023-10-12 09:00:06,10.0.0.5,8.8.8.8,ICMP,0,DROP
2023-10-12 09:00:07,10.0.0.5,1.1.1.1,ICMP,0,ACCEPT
2023-10-12 09:00:08,10.0.0.5,8.8.4.4,ICMP,0,REJECT
2023-10-12 09:00:09,10.0.0.5,8.8.8.8,ICMP,0,ACCEPT
2023-10-12 09:00:10,10.0.0.5,8.8.8.8,ICMP,0,ACCEPT
        </file>
    </preexisting_items>

    <expected_outputs>
        <!-- 1 -->
        <file path="/home/user/network/reports/connection_status_frequency.txt" mode="0644" owner="user">
ACCEPT 6
DROP 3
REJECT 1
        </file>

        <!-- 2 -->
        <file path="/home/user/network/reports/ip_failure_frequency.txt" mode="0644" owner="user">
8.8.4.4 2
8.8.8.8 2
        </file>
    </expected_outputs>
